epigastric.f: Abdominal auras associated with TLE in 74%; rising to 98% when the seizure evolves into an automotor component.

In an SEEG-defined series (n=80: 58 pure temporal, 22 temporal-'plus'), digestive/abdominal auras were significantly more frequent in pure temporal than temporal-'plus' seizures (54.2% vs 30.4%, p=0.05); coexistence of abdominal plus pharyngeal auras clustered together as a marker of the pure-temporal group.

oral automatisms.f: Unilateral automatisms are typically ipsilateral to the epileptogenic zone; strongest when paired with contralateral dystonic posturing.

unilateral manual.f: Unilateral automatisms tend to be ipsilateral to the EZ; the pairing 'ipsilateral automatism + contralateral dystonia' is the classic lateralizing dyad.
In R-vs-L TLE, ipsilateral limb automatisms combined with contralateral posturing best separated the groups; contralateral posturing tends to inhibit ipsilateral automatisms.

contralateral dystonic.f: Unilateral dystonic posturing occurs in 43.9% of TLE and is contralateral to the EZ in ~100% (single exception reported by Yen); mechanism = basal-ganglia activation.
Limb dystonia is contralateral to the EZ in >90% of cases.

Sustained (>10 sec) forced dystonic posturing of one upper extremity with a rotational component is a reliable lateralizing sign to the contralateral hemisphere in TLE (also seen in extratemporal epilepsy); ictal SPECT confirms basal-ganglia involvement.

figure-of-4.f: In secondarily-generalized seizures the extended elbow was contralateral to onset in 94.4% (extratemporal) and 83–88% (temporal); asymmetric tonic limb posturing present in 75% of TLE and 53% of extratemporal 2°-GTC seizures.
Figure-of-4 present in 17.7% of TLE / 15% of extratemporal epilepsy; extended arm contralateral in 89%.
Caution: in a systematic video-PPV analysis, figure-of-4 and hand dystonia had PPV <80% — less reliable in isolation than version, unilateral tonic posturing, M2e, or unilateral clonic activity.
The figure-of-4 sign is listed as contralateral to the seizure focus in the lateralizing-signs summary table.

late forced.f: Forced version present in 22.2% of FLE; contralateral to the EZ in 100% (Brodmann 6/8), especially the pre-GTCS versive turn.
Forced version occurred exclusively on the contralateral side in 89% of seizures.

Head/eye deviation accompanied by true forced version is contralateral to the seizure-onset zone; head rotation without a versive component tends to be ipsilateral — the versive quality, not the turn itself, carries the lateralizing value.

Foundational series (37 patients, 74 seizures): versive (forced, involuntary, sustained) head/eye deviation was contralateral to the focus in every lateralizing instance — contralateral versive in 61 seizures / 27 patients, with ipsilateral versive never observed — whereas non-versive turning occurred ipsi- and contralaterally with equal frequency and was non-localizing.

Prior video-EEG series (Ochs 1984, Robillard 1983) had scored ipsiversion in 48-50% of seizures and concluded head turning had no lateralizing value; Wyllie attributes the discrepancy to their more permissive definition of 'versive' that included mild/ambiguous rotations. When only marked, sustained, unambiguous forced turning was scored as versive, ipsiversion was never observed (0/61).

Version's temporal relationship to other ictal signs differed by lobe of onset: 35/61 (57%) versive seizures began with the versive movement itself, and 91% of these were frontal/extratemporal; none were temporal. All 18 seizures in which staring-plus-automatisms preceded contraversion (30% of the versive cohort) arose from temporal onset, and contraversion never occurred as the initial sign in a temporal-onset seizure.

Of the 27/61 (44%) versive seizures that secondarily generalized, 12 (44%) ended with ipsiversion during or after the generalized convulsion - only the first, pre-generalization versive movement reliably lateralizes; turning observed during/after secondary generalization can reverse side and should not be used for lateralization.

Interictal EEG foci among the 27 patients with versive seizures were exclusively contralateral to the versive direction in 81% (22/27) and never exclusively ipsilateral (5/27 bilateral), corroborating the ictal contralateral-version finding.

Versive seizures (forced, involuntary head/eye turning) have high lateralizing value to the contralateral hemisphere, especially when occurring within 10 seconds before secondary generalization; reliability requires clearly differentiating them from non-versive head turning.

Contralateral version of the eyes and/or head was significantly more frequent in temporal-'plus' than pure temporal lobe seizures (60.9% vs 35.6% overall, p=0.04; contralateral subtype specifically p=0.001); when contralateral version occurs in an apparently temporal seizure, especially with accompanying anxiety, it should raise suspicion of temporal-'plus' rather than pure TLE.

early non-forced.f: Non-forced head deviation was contralateral in 63% and ipsilateral in 35% — weak and not statistically significant, unlike forced version.

Nonversive (mild, unsustained, wandering, or seemingly voluntary) head/eye turning occurred in 13 seizures from 10 patients and was split almost evenly between ipsilateral (7 seizures/4 patients) and contralateral (6 seizures/6 patients) to seizure onset, confirming it carries no lateralizing value.

automatism + contralateral.f: The ipsilateral-automatism-plus-contralateral-posturing pattern differentiated right from left TLE; contralateral posturing inhibits ipsilateral automatisms.

preserved responsiveness.f: Automatisms with preserved responsiveness occur in 5.7% of TLE and lateralize to the non-dominant hemisphere in ~100% (one exception, Janszky).
Preserved consciousness during automatisms supports a non-dominant temporal origin.

Lateralizing-signs table lists automatism with preserved awareness ('automotor without dialepsis') as localizing to the non-dominant hemisphere, and automatism with impaired awareness ('automotor with dialepsis') as localizing to the dominant hemisphere.

95% of automotor seizures are associated with altered consciousness; preserved consciousness during automotor seizures has been observed almost exclusively in patients with non-dominant mesial temporal epilepsy.

ictal spitting.f: Ictal spitting occurs in ~0.3% of monitored patients; non-dominant in 75%.
Ictal spitting is listed as localizing to the non-dominant hemisphere, flagged 'not highly reliable.'

ictus emeticus.f: Ictal vomiting in ~2% of monitored patients; non-dominant (right) in 81% (14/16 right-lateralized); non-dominant temporal + Papez circuit.
Abdominal aura combined with vomiting further increases the probability of temporal (non-dominant) origin.
Ictal emesis is listed as localizing to the non-dominant hemisphere, flagged 'not highly reliable.'

eye blinking.f: Unilateral ictal eye-blinking in ~1.5% of monitored patients; ipsilateral to the EZ in 83%.
Unilateral eye-blinking occurred ipsilateral to ictal EEG onset in 85.7%.
Unilateral ictal eye blinking is listed as ipsilateral to the seizure focus; the table lists it twice, once as a standard entry and again flagged 'not highly reliable.'
Unilateral eye blinking is infrequent but has been reported as a good lateralizing sign to the ipsilateral hemisphere; it has no localizing value and its mechanism is unclear.

postictal nose.f: Postictal nose-wiping in 53.2% of TLE; the wiping hand is ipsilateral to the EZ in 92%.

Post-ictal nose wiping lateralizes to the ipsilateral hemisphere in TLE, attributed to post-ictal neglect of the space contralateral to the wiping hand (i.e., contralateral to the seizure-onset hemisphere).

postictal aphasia.f: Postictal dysphasia lateralized to the dominant temporal lobe in 92% (p<0.001).
Postictal dysphasia/aphasia is dominant-hemisphere in 80–100% across series.
Classic n=100 anterior-temporal-lobectomy series establishing dysphasia with dominant-temporal seizure origin.
Critical appraisal: 'epileptic aphasia' is heterogeneous — post-ictal aphasia is a robust dominant-hemisphere sign, but ictal language phenomena require careful, structured testing to interpret.
Postictal aphasia is listed as localizing to the dominant hemisphere in the lateralizing-signs table.

Postictal aphasia lateralizes to the language-dominant hemisphere in TLE; recovery of language function is significantly more delayed with left (dominant) temporal onset, and should progress slowly (10-20 min) but continuously in a cooperative, testable patient.

ictal aphasia.f: Ictal dysphasia/aphasia lateralized to the dominant hemisphere in ~100%; ictal preserved (identifiable) speech to the non-dominant side in 83%.
Ictal speech that is preserved/identifiable is non-dominant in 83%; ictal dysphasia is dominant in 100%.

In an alternate copy of the lateralizing-signs table (p.7), 'ictal speech' (i.e. preserved/intelligible speech during a seizure) is listed as localizing to the non-dominant hemisphere; the more complete copy of the table (p.5) omits this row, listing 'immediate postictal speech' instead.

Ictal verbalization (clearly intelligible speech during unresponsiveness/automatisms) tends to lateralize to the non-dominant hemisphere in TLE, but exceptions are frequent, making it a poorly reliable lateralizing sign; pure vocalization has no lateralizing value.

ictal aphasia / speech arrest.f: Ictal dysphasia dominant ~100%; preserved ictal speech non-dominant 83%.

paraphasia.f: Paraphasic/abnormal ictal speech carries dominant-hemisphere lateralizing value; abnormal speech occurred (ictally or postictally) in 51.4% of patients studied.
Ictal paraphasia is an uncommon but dominant-lateralizing language sign requiring structured ictal testing to capture.

focal clonic.f: Unilateral clonic activity in 44.4% of FLE; contralateral to the EZ in 83% (Brodmann 4/6).
Unilateral clonic activity is a 'reliable' motor sign (PPV >80%) for contralateral lateralization.

Unilateral clonic seizures have a highly lateralizing value to the contralateral hemisphere; the epileptogenic zone is usually at or near the primary motor strip when clonus without impaired consciousness is the first ictal sign.

focal hand/finger clonic.f: Unilateral clonic activity contralateral in 83%; hand representation most common.

todd.f: Postictal (Todd's) palsy in ~0.6% of monitored patients; contralateral to the EZ in 93%.

postictal unilateral limb.f: Postictal paresis is contralateral to the EZ in 93% (Todd's palsy data).

bilateral asymmetric tonic.f: Tonic activity in 48.1% of FLE; the asymmetric (extended) side is contralateral in 89% (SMA / Brodmann 6).
Unilateral tonic posturing is a 'reliable' sign (PPV >80%); two reliable signs pointing the same way give ~100% lateralization accuracy.

Tonic seizures occur far more often in frontal lobe epilepsy (62.2%) than temporal lobe epilepsy (1.7%); only unilateral tonic seizures occur in TLE, whereas 32% of FLE tonic seizures are bilateral. Clearly unilateral tonic seizures carry high lateralizing significance, contralateral to seizure onset.

somatosensory aura.f: Unilateral sensory aura in 6.1% of patients; contralateral in 89% (Brodmann 1/2/3).

ictal pain.f: Ictal pain occurs in 0.2–2.8% of people with epilepsy (up to 4.1% in focal epilepsy). Somatosensory pain → parietal operculum / insula; abdominal pain → temporal & parietal foci (amygdala/insula). Contralateral when somatotopically organized.

elementary visual.f: Hemifield visual aura in 28.6% of occipital-lobe epilepsy; contralateral hemifield in 100% (Brodmann 17–19).

m2e.f: M2e (mouth-to-hand, Ajmone-Marsan) is a 'reliable' motor sign (PPV >80%) with strong contralateral value — provided the contralateral arm is the one moving to the mouth.

hypermotor.f: French SEEG analysis maps hypermotor behaviour along a rostro-caudal frontal gradient; ventromedial-prefrontal/orbitofrontal onset yields the most distal, integrated hyperkinetic behaviours.

head turning.f: Early (exploratory, non-forced) head deviation is listed as ipsilateral to the seizure focus, flagged 'not highly reliable.'

eye deviation.f: Forced eye version is listed as contralateral to the seizure focus in the lateralizing-signs table.

head version.f: Forced head version is listed as contralateral to the seizure focus in the lateralizing-signs table.

dystonic.f: Ictal dystonia is listed as contralateral to the seizure focus in the lateralizing-signs table.

vocalization.f: Ictal vocalization is listed as localizing to the dominant hemisphere, flagged 'not highly reliable.'

unilateral manual automatisms.f: Unilateral ictal automatisms are listed as ipsilateral to the seizure focus, flagged 'not highly reliable.'

tongue biting.f: Lateral tongue biting is listed as ipsilateral to the seizure focus — the opposite direction from this atlas's current contralateral label on the matching sign, worth reconciling against other sources.

m2e automatism.f: The M2e sign is listed as contralateral to the seizure focus (the hand moving to the mouth is contralateral) in the lateralizing-signs table.

peri-ictal headache.f: Peri-ictal headache is listed as ipsilateral to the seizure focus, flagged 'not highly reliable.'

postictal paralysis.f: Postictal hemiparesis is listed as contralateral to the seizure focus in the lateralizing-signs table.

postictal nose wiping.f: Postictal nose wiping is listed as ipsilateral to the seizure focus, flagged 'not highly reliable.'

urinary urge.f: Peri-ictal urinary urge is listed as localizing to the non-dominant hemisphere, flagged 'not highly reliable.'

asymmetric terminal.f: In secondarily generalized tonic-clonic seizures, clonic activity may persist longer on the side ipsilateral to the epileptogenic focus ('end-of-seizure paradoxical clonus'), attributed to earlier exhaustion of the seizure-onset hemisphere; described as a highly reliable lateralizing sign.

drinking automatism.f: Peri-ictal water drinking has been reported as a lateralizing sign to the non-dominant temporal lobe, though its validity has been contested in later work.

gustatory aura.f: Gustatory hallucinations were significantly more frequent in temporal-'plus' than pure temporal lobe seizures (21.7% vs 5.1%, p=0.02), and specifically associated with the temporo-sylvian (insulo-opercular) temporal-'plus' subgroup (p=0.009).

vertiginous aura.f: Vestibular illusions, particularly rotatory vertigo, were significantly more frequent in temporal-'plus' than pure temporal lobe seizures (13% vs 1.7% overall, p=0.03; rotatory subtype p=0.02), and specifically associated with the temporo-parieto-occipital (TPO) temporal-'plus' subgroup (p=0.02).

auditory illusions.f: Auditory illusions (but not auditory hallucinations) were significantly more frequent in temporal-'plus' than pure temporal lobe seizures (p=0.02), consistent with the hypothesis that illusions reflect more widely extended superior-temporal-gyrus discharge while hallucinations reflect more spatially limited discharge.

Unlike other auditory-seizure subtypes, illusions (sound distortions) showed no lateralizing tendency — cases arose about equally from left- and right-hemisphere onset zones — and, when reported individually, illusions were uniformly perceived bilaterally rather than in one ear.

ictal piloerection.f: Piloerection was significantly more frequent in temporal-'plus' than pure temporal lobe seizures (13% vs 1.7%, p=0.03), suggesting more extended involvement of the central autonomic network at seizure onset.

simple auditory aura.f: In a compiled review of 174 cases/200 auditory seizures, simple auditory hallucinations (meaningless noises, e.g. buzzing/ringing) were mostly unilateral and, when heard in one ear, most often contralateral to the seizure-onset zone; only 9% of their temporal SOZs were in Heschl's gyrus, with the superior temporal gyrus (38%) more often implicated.

Across 174 compiled cases (200 auditory seizures), about 70% had a temporal seizure-onset zone (most often superior temporal gyrus or mesiotemporal structures), but roughly 30% arose from extratemporal structures — chiefly the inferior frontal gyrus (frontal lobe), inferior parietal lobule/postcentral gyrus (parietal lobe), and insula — so an auditory aura should not be assumed to localize strictly to the temporal lobe.

auditory verbal hallucinations.f: Auditory verbal hallucinations (voices) lateralized inconsistently across the reviewed literature, with only a slight left-hemisphere predominance (60% of cases); when heard unilaterally they were more often contralateral to the seizure-onset zone, and nearly half of their temporal-lobe onsets localized to mesiotemporal structures rather than lateral temporal cortex.

complex auditory hallucinations.f: Complex auditory hallucinations (formed sounds, e.g. tram/engine noise) tended to be contralateral to the seizure-onset zone, though the number of reported cases was small; their seizure-onset zones localized mainly to the superior temporal gyrus and mesiotemporal structures.

new_signs.notes: Recurrent ictal speech utterances with abnormal melody/intonation (dysprosody) rather than aphasic content. Intracranial EEG localizes the discharge to the NON-dominant precentral operculum — the prosodic mirror of dominant-hemisphere language cortex. Helps lateralize to the non-dominant frontal operculum when speech is altered but not aphasic.

_ev.f: Ictal recurrent speech with altered prosody corresponded to ictal discharge of the non-dominant precentral operculum, giving dysprosody localizing value to the non-dominant frontal operculum.

new_signs.loc: Mesial temporal — amygdala / peri-amygdalar central-apnea network
new_signs.spec: ~82% (0.95 when followed by focal impaired awareness)
new_signs.notes: Objective cessation of airflow AND respiratory effort during a focal seizure — distinct from the subjective dyspnea / respiratory-distress aura. Strongly predicts MESIAL TEMPORAL onset: OR 3.8 (specificity 0.82, sensitivity 0.45); when it is followed by focal impaired awareness the odds rise ~30-fold (specificity 0.95). No lateral-temporal or insular onsets showed it. Occurs in ~17.5% of focal seizures / 39% of patients, who have more temporal-lobe onset (85% vs 52%). The amygdala ipsilateral to the epileptogenic zone is enlarged in affected patients (a structural, not lateralizing, correlate). Clinically important as a SUDEP-relevant sign.
new_signs.cite: Lacuey et al., Ann Neurol 2024; Meletti et al., Neurology 2025

_ev.f: Ictal central apnea predicted mesial temporal seizure onset (OR 3.8, specificity 0.82, sensitivity 0.45); when followed by focal impaired awareness, specificity rose to 0.95; no lateral-temporal or insular onsets showed it.
Ictal central apnea in 17.5% of focal seizures / 39% of patients; patients with it had higher temporal-lobe onset than those without (85% vs 52%, p=0.01).

new_signs.loc: Mesial temporal; downstream of ictal central apnea; ipsilateral amygdala enlargement
new_signs.notes: Central apnea persisting AFTER seizure end. Occurs in 5.9% of focal seizures and 33.8% of seizures that had ictal central apnea; never observed without a preceding or concurrent ictal central apnea. Associated with temporal-lobe epilepsy and with an enlarged amygdala ipsilateral to the epileptogenic zone. Established mainly as a SUDEP risk marker (longer postictal central apnea → higher hazard of sudden death) rather than a validated lateralizing sign.
new_signs.cite: Meletti et al., Neurology 2025; Ochoa-Urrea et al., Lancet 2025

_ev.f: Postictal central apnea in 24/406 (5.9%) focal seizures and 24/71 (33.8%) of seizures with ictal central apnea; never without a preceding/concurrent ictal central apnea; associated with TLE and ipsilateral amygdala enlargement.
Longer postictal central apnea was a risk marker for sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (hazard ratio 1.32 per 10 s; cutoff >14 s).

new_signs.notes: Automatic drinking (reaching for and sipping water) during or just after a focal impaired-awareness seizure, reported as more common with right (non-dominant) temporal foci. Belongs to the cluster of right-temporal autonomic automatisms alongside ictal spitting and vomiting. Corroborative rather than definitive in isolation.

_ev.f: Spitting and drinking automatisms suggest right temporal localization; ictal spitting, ictal flatulence, and ictal drinking are more common with right temporal foci.

new_signs.notes: Coughing shortly after seizure termination, found predominantly following right (non-dominant) temporal-lobe seizures. One of several right-temporal autonomic/postictal lateralizing signs; not sufficient in isolation but contributes when combined with other signs.

_ev.f: Post-ictal cough has been found predominantly following right temporal seizures.

new_signs.notes: An urge to urinate after a seizure, suggesting a right (non-dominant) temporal localization. A right-temporal autonomic lateralizing sign; corroborative, to be weighed together with other signs rather than used alone.

_ev.f: Post-ictal urinary urgency suggests a right temporal localization.

new_signs.sign: Asymmetric terminal seizure activity (ending clonus/version ipsilateral to focus)
new_signs.loc: Reflects the seizure-onset hemisphere at the moment the seizure is ending, distinct from the earlier forced-version phase
new_signs.spec: Not quantified in source; listed among the table's standard (non-starred, i.e. not flagged unreliable) entries
new_signs.notes: Teaching-table entry distinguishing the terminal (ending) asymmetric motor activity of a seizure — including terminal clonic jerking and any late versive movement — from the earlier, contralateral forced version seen during the tonic/versive phase. Listed as ipsilateral to the seizure focus, the mirror image of early forced version's contralateral value.

_ev.f: Lateralizing-signs table lists 'asymmetric ending seizure' (including terminal clonus and version) as ipsilateral to the seizure focus.

new_signs.sign: Immediate postictal speech (rapid resumption of speech right after seizure end)
new_signs.loc: Non-dominant hemisphere (mirror of dominant-hemisphere postictal aphasia)
new_signs.notes: Rapid resumption of fluent speech immediately after a seizure favors a NON-dominant hemisphere seizure - dominant-hemisphere seizures more often leave postictal aphasia / delayed speech return. From a fellowship-handbook lateralizing-signs table; low-corroboration, weigh with other signs. (Corrected on maintainer review to the conventional non-dominant direction; the initial photo-table extraction had recorded it as dominant.)

_ev.f: Fellowship-handbook lateralizing-signs table entry for immediate/rapid return of speech after seizure end; localizes to the non-dominant hemisphere (the mirror of postictal aphasia, which is dominant).

new_signs.spec: Low reliability - flagged unreliable in the source table
new_signs.notes: LOW-RELIABILITY lateralizing sign - kept for completeness, not for standalone use. Unilateral pupillary dilation during a seizure is listed in the handbook's lateralizing-signs table as localizing to the CONTRALATERAL frontal lobe, but the table itself flags it as 'not highly reliable.' Corroborate before relying on it.

_ev.f: Unilateral pupillary dilatation is listed as localizing to the contralateral frontal lobe, and explicitly flagged 'not highly reliable', in the lateralizing-signs table.

new_signs.loc: Non-dominant hemisphere (autonomic/urge sensation); no lobar localization given in source
new_signs.spec: Low reliability - flagged unreliable in the source table
new_signs.notes: LOW-RELIABILITY lateralizing sign - kept for completeness, not for standalone use. A peri-ictal urge to urinate is listed in the handbook's lateralizing-signs table as localizing to the non-dominant hemisphere, but the table flags it 'not highly reliable.' Corroborate before relying on it.

_ev.f: Peri-ictal urinary urge is listed as localizing to the non-dominant hemisphere, and explicitly flagged 'not highly reliable', in the lateralizing-signs table.

new_signs.lat: Non-lateralizing (marks network extension beyond the temporal lobe)
new_signs.loc: Marks epileptogenic-network extension beyond mesial/lateral temporal lobe into orbitofrontal, insular, opercular, or temporo-parieto-occipital cortex ('temporal-plus' epilepsy), rather than pure temporal lobe epilepsy
new_signs.notes: SEEG-defined series (n=80: 58 pure temporal, 22 temporal-'plus'). Post-ictal dysphoric mood change (depression or euphoria) was far more common after temporal-'plus' seizures than after pure temporal lobe seizures — the single strongest discriminator between the two groups in this series. Not itself a lateralizing sign, but a localizing marker that the epileptogenic network extends beyond the temporal lobe proper, relevant to deciding whether invasive (SEEG) evaluation is needed before temporal lobe surgery.

_ev.f: Post-ictal dysphoric state occurred in 39.1% of temporal-'plus' seizures vs 6.8% of pure temporal lobe seizures (p=0.0001), the single strongest discriminator between the two groups.

new_signs.sign: Ability to warn at seizure onset (preserved aura reporting)
new_signs.lat: Non-lateralizing (marks a more restricted, purely temporal onset)
new_signs.loc: Preserved speech/consciousness during the aura reflects a more restricted (purely temporal, often mesial) ictal onset; earlier loss of this ability suggests a larger, temporal-'plus' network activated from seizure onset
new_signs.notes: SEEG-defined series (n=80). Patients with pure temporal lobe epilepsy were far more able to signal/report their seizure onset (aura) than those with temporal-'plus' epilepsy, despite auras occurring at similar overall frequency in both groups (88.7% overall) — consistent with temporal-plus seizures activating a larger cortical network and impairing consciousness earlier.

_ev.f: Ability to warn at seizure onset was present in 78% of pure temporal lobe seizures vs 43.5% of temporal-'plus' seizures (p=0.003), despite similar overall aura frequency in both groups.

papers: Lateralizing signs during seizures in focal epilepsy
Comprehensive lateralizing-sign review; source of the frequency + lateralization-% table used throughout.
Localizing and lateralizing features of auras and seizures
Aura/seizure localization review; abdominal-aura → TLE probabilities and motor-sign lateralization.
Ictal motor sequences: lateralization and localization values
PPV-based ranking of motor signs and the sequence rule (≥2 reliable signs → ~100% accuracy).
Lateralizing value of asymmetric tonic limb posturing in secondarily generalized seizures
Figure-of-4 / ATLP quantification: extended elbow contralateral in up to 94%.
Lateralizing / localizing value of seizure semiology (update)
Contemporary synthesis of semiological lateralizing signs.
Speech manifestations in lateralization of temporal lobe seizures
Postictal dysphasia → dominant 92%; preserved ictal speech → non-dominant 83%.
Speech disturbances in temporal lobe seizures (n=100 ATL)
Classic large series linking ictal dysphasia to the dominant temporal lobe.
Ictal dysprosody and the non-dominant frontal operculum
Establishes dysprosody as a non-dominant frontal-opercular sign.
Critical synthesis of ictal/postictal language phenomena and their localizing limits.
Differentiating clinical features of right and left temporal lobe seizures
Lateralizing value of ictal behaviors in temporal lobe epilepsy
Version contralateral 89%; eye-blink & unilateral automatism ipsilateral ~85%.
Frontal lobe seizures: from clinical semiology to localization
French SEEG cohort mapping frontal semiology along a rostro-caudal gradient.
Value of semiology in predicting EZ and outcome in frontal lobe surgery
Semiology's predictive value for the frontal EZ and surgical outcome.
Emergence of semiology in epileptic seizures
Network/dynamic framework: semiology reflects dynamic network interaction, not propagation alone.
Conceptual framework linking semiology to organized brain networks.
Frontal lobe seizures: overview and update
Modern overview of frontal-lobe seizure semiology and SEEG correlation.
Methodological standard for stereo-EEG-based anatomo-electro-clinical correlation.
Semiologic and electrophysiologic correlations in temporal lobe seizure subtypes
SEEG-defined mesial/lateral/mesiolateral temporal subtypes and their semiology.
Painful seizures: a review of epileptic ictal pain
Prevalence and network basis of ictal pain (parietal operculum / insula).
Structured testing during seizures: a practical guide
European consensus ictal–postictal testing battery (how to elicit/verify each sign).
SEEG language-mapping methods relevant to ictal language signs.
Clinical features of sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy vs seizure-onset zone
Semiology of SHE in relation to onset zone.
Malformations of cortical development and epilepsy
Structural substrate context for focal epilepsy.
Lesional/EZ concordance in focal dysplasia (pediatric context).
Population context for focal epilepsy syndromes.
Localisation in focal epilepsy: a practical guide
Ictal speech disturbance and cerebral dominance
Ictal paraphasia as an atypical TLE manifestation
The lateralizing significance of versive head and eye movements during epileptic seizures
Foundational series: versive (forced, sustained) head/eye deviation is reliably contralateral; non-versive turning is non-localizing.
Ictal central apnea is predictive of mesial temporal seizure onsets
Intracranial study: ictal central apnea predicts mesial temporal onset (OR 3.8, spec 0.82).
Persistent postictal central apnea in focal seizures: incidence, features, and imaging findings
Postictal central apnea incidence/features; TLE association and ipsilateral amygdala enlargement.
Risk markers for sudden unexpected death in epilepsy: an observational, prospective, multicentre cohort study
SUDEP cohort: ictal/postictal central-apnea duration as mortality risk markers.
Atlas of EEG, Seizure Semiology, and Management, 3rd ed. — Seizure semiology by localization (ch 3.4)

Authoritative textbook chapter corroborating the lateralizing direction of version, dystonic/tonic/clonic posturing, figure-of-4, somatosensory, spitting, vomiting, preserved-responsiveness automatisms, eye-blinking, nose-wiping and postictal aphasia.
Epilepsy Fellow Handbook — localizing/lateralizing seizure semiology reference tables

UTHealth McGovern Medical School epilepsy fellowship teaching handbook; bedside quick-reference tables of aura/semiology subtypes and a lateralizing-signs summary (ipsilateral/contralateral/dominant/nondominant), each entry flagged for reliability. Treated as a secondary/teaching synthesis, not a primary series with its own cohort.
Seizure Semiology: Its Value and Limitations in Localizing the Epileptogenic Zone

Review by Tufenkjian & Lüders synthesizing localizing/lateralizing seizure signs by symptomatogenic zone — tonic/clonic/versive/dystonic motor signs, ictal vs postictal speech, nose-wiping, eye-blinking, peri-ictal drinking, and end-of-seizure paradoxical clonus.
Ictal clinical and scalp-EEG findings differentiating temporal lobe epilepsies from temporal 'plus' epilepsies

SEEG-defined series (n=80: 58 pure temporal, 22 temporal-'plus') identifying ictal signs — gustatory/vestibular/auditory-illusion auras, piloerection, ipsilateral tonic signs, contralateral version, postictal dysphoria — that mark temporal-'plus' network extension (orbitofrontal/insula/operculum/TPO) vs pure temporal lobe epilepsy.
Localizing and lateralizing value of auditory phenomena in seizures

Narrative review compiling 174 cases / 200 auditory seizures from 70 articles, classified by auditory-phenomenon subtype (simple/complex hallucination, illusion, verbal/musical hallucination); most auditory seizures have a temporal SOZ (70%) but ~30% are extratemporal (frontal/parietal/insular).
